# Cannabis Dispensary Permit — Compliance Requirements Matrix

**Applicant:** Greenleaf Therapeutics LLC ("Greenleaf")
**Proposed Location:** 1420 Sherman Ave., Unit B, Evanston, IL 60201 (C1a Commercial Mixed-Use district)
**State Conditional License:** ADC-285-0147 (issued Sept. 22, 2023; expires Sept. 21, 2024)
**Application Window:** February 1, 2024 – May 31, 2024 (target submission: May 15, 2024)
**Governing Authorities Reviewed:** Evanston Municipal Code, Title 9, Ch. 9, Art. 5 ("Article 5" or "the Code"); City of Evanston *Cannabis Dispensary Permit Application Checklist and Submission Guide* (Jan. 15, 2024) ("the Guide"); City of Evanston *Cannabis Dispensary Permit Application* Form v3.2 (Jan. 12, 2024) ("the Form")
**Prepared:** Compliance review of the submitted application package

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This matrix assesses the Greenleaf Therapeutics LLC dispensary permit application package against every distinct requirement of Article 5 of the Evanston Municipal Code and the City's Application Checklist and Submission Guide. Each requirement is scored on its own line with a status, the evidentiary basis in the record, and a remediation note.

**Overarching finding — the application form itself is unexecuted.** The *Cannabis Dispensary Permit Application* (Form v3.2) included in the package is a blank template: every field in Sections 1–14 is uncompleted, the certification block (Section 14) is unsigned, and the notarization block is unexecuted. Although the supporting documents (business plan, lease summary, insurance/bonding memo, community-engagement report, and State conditional license) contain much of the underlying substance, the Code (§9-5-5(B)(1)) and the Guide (Item 1) require a *completed, signed, and notarized* application form. Until the Form is filled in, signed, and notarized, the package cannot be deemed complete. This single gap propagates across nearly every line item below and is reflected in the "Form blank" notes throughout.

**Critical substantive deficiencies (would render the application incomplete or materially weaken it):**

1. **Odor Mitigation Plan — no P.E. certification and no engineered specifications.** The Form (Q16) and Guide (Item 10) require a Professional Engineer's certification, signed and sealed by an Illinois-licensed P.E., *at the time of submission*, and the Form expressly states the application "will be deemed incomplete and will not proceed to scoring" without it. The business plan instead defers all HVAC/carbon-filtration specifications to "the build-out engineering phase" and provides no P.E. certification. *(Non-compliant.)*
2. **Security Plan not finalized.** The insurance/bonding memo confirms the Pinnacle Compliance Advisors security plan is "still being finalized … and has not yet been delivered." The business plan contains only an overview. A finalized security plan is a required submission item (Item 9) and is a prerequisite to binding the cannabis crime/theft policy. *(Non-compliant.)*
3. **Cannabis-Specific Crime/Theft insurance — not bound, no binder/commitment letter.** The Guide (Item 16) permits a binding commitment letter at application stage; only a "preliminary indication" from Canopy Risk Underwriters exists, and underwriting is blocked pending the security plan. *(Non-compliant at application; blocking for permit issuance.)*
4. **No evidence of bonding capacity.** Only a "preliminary inquiry" with Thornfield Surety Group; no commitment letter. The Form's scoring rubric awards 10 of 30 Financial Viability points for secured bonding capacity and states applicants who do not provide evidence "will receive zero (0) points in this sub-category." Guide Item 17 also requires evidence of bonding capacity at submission. *(Non-compliant; scoring penalty.)*
5. **Community-engagement Session 2 notice was only 12 days.** Notice for the April 9, 2024 session was published March 28, 2024 — 12 calendar days, below the 14-day minimum of Code §9-5-8(B). The engagement report itself records "12 days" and omits the compliance statement it supplied for Session 1. Code §9-5-8(F) renders non-compliant notice a completeness defect and bars waiver of the two-session minimum. *(Non-compliant; a third, properly-noticed session is advisable.)*
6. **Buffer-distance certification is stale and uses the wrong methodology.** The Meridian survey is dated Dec. 15, 2023 (the Form requires surveys dated within 60 days of submission; a May 15, 2024 filing makes it ~5 months old) and measures *straight-line* distances, while the Form (Q6/Q7) expressly requires *nearest-pedestrian-route* measurement and rejects straight-line. The underlying distances (school 1,180 ft; daycare 890 ft; dispensary 2,100 ft) all satisfy the Code minimums, but the certification as submitted does not satisfy the Form. *(Non-compliant.)*
7. **State conditional-license expiration timeline risk.** The conditional license expires Sept. 21, 2024. The City's own 120–150 day estimate from a May 15, 2024 submission places City Council action as late as mid-October 2024 — *after* the conditional license expires — and the 180-day conversion clock cannot start until local permit issuance. No State extension petition (available up to 6 months if filed ≥60 days before expiration, i.e., by ~July 23, 2024) is documented. *(Critical timeline risk.)*
8. **No standalone Diversity & Inclusion Plan or Environmental Sustainability Plan.** Both are required as standalone submission items (Guide Items 14, 15; Form Q32/Q33). Only business-plan narrative exists; the social-equity hiring pipeline identifies no formalized workforce-development partners, no timeline, and no contingency plan as required by Code §9-5-20(C). *(Non-compliant / partial.)*
9. **Social-equity supporting documentation not submitted.** R3 residency proof (with census-tract verification and 24-month duration), the expungement court order, and the signed 50%-R3-hiring and 2-year-mentorship commitments (Guide Item 18) are not in the package; the Form's social-equity sections (Q19–Q21) are blank. The 24-month continuous R3 residency required by Code §9-5-19(B)(1) is not documented. *(Not submitted.)*

**Cross-cutting issue — internal inconsistencies among the Code, the Guide, and the Form.** The three governing documents conflict on several points that directly affect preparation: the deficiency cure period (Code 30 days vs. Guide/Form 14 days); acceptable fee instruments; the Certificate of Good Standing freshness window (60 vs. 30 days); the list of qualifying zoning districts (Code: C1a/C2/oC1 vs. Form: C1a/C1/B2); the maximum-occupancy calculation base (Code: retail floor area vs. Form: total dispensary area); the operating-hours citation (Form cites §9-5-17; the rule is §9-5-13(F)); Employer's Liability limits (Code $1M vs. lease $500K vs. Guide/Form silent); the SSN disclosure (Code: full vs. Form: last 4); Agent-in-Charge availability (Code: 30-minute return vs. Form: by telephone); the social-equity workforce definition; and the felony lookback period (City 10 years vs. State 5 years); an internal Code conflict on the insurance-lapse immediate-suspension threshold (§9-5-16(E): 14 days vs. §9-5-25(C)(3): 48 hours); and a citation error in the Form (Q22 cites §9-5-15, the community-benefit section, for the community-engagement requirement, which is actually §9-5-8). These are catalogued in Section 5 and Section 6 and should be reconciled with the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator before filing.

**Data inconsistencies within the package** (registered-agent name, applicant telephone/email, parking-space count, City contact coordinates) are catalogued in Section 7 and must be reconciled across documents before submission.

---

## 2. Scope and Methodology

- **Records reviewed:** (1) Evanston Municipal Code Ch. 9, Art. 5; (2) Application Checklist and Submission Guide; (3) Cannabis Dispensary Permit Application Form v3.2; (4) Greenleaf Business Plan Summary; (5) Community Engagement Sessions Report; (6) Insurance & Bonding Status Memo; (7) Lease Summary — Key Terms; (8) State Conditional Adult-Use Dispensing Organization License ADC-285-0147.
- **Unit of analysis:** each distinct statutory/guide requirement is assessed on its own line. Where the Code, Guide, and Form impose overlapping or differing versions of a requirement, each material variant is captured separately so no graded point is bundled.
- **Status vocabulary:** **COMPLIANT** (requirement met, evidence in record); **PARTIAL** (some elements met, gaps remain); **NON-COMPLIANT** (requirement violated or not met); **NOT SUBMITTED** (required item absent from package); **NOT CONFIRMED** (record does not evidence the required fact); **PENDING** (obligation arises at/after permit issuance; acknowledged but not yet due); **DISCREPANCY** (conflict among governing documents); **N/A** (not applicable at application stage).
- **Citations:** "Code §9-5-X" = Municipal Code; "Guide Item N" = Submission Guide Master Checklist; "Form QN" = Application Form question. Supporting-document references use the document name and section.

15. **Revise the floor plan and square-footage breakdown to include restrooms** (and any mechanical rooms), and confirm the 1,600-sq-ft retail figure excludes them per §9-5-2(O) (ID 183). The current breakdown omits restrooms, a required functional area under §9-5-5(B)(6).

## 7. Remediation Action Plan — Sequenced Next Steps

**Immediately (before May 15, 2024 submission):**

1. Complete, sign, and notarize the Application Form; transcribe all supporting-document content into the appropriate Form sections.
2. Engage an Illinois-licensed P.E. to design and certify the odor-mitigation system; produce the signed/sealed certification and specific HVAC/carbon-filtration specifications.
3. Direct Pinnacle Compliance Advisors to finalize and deliver the complete Security Plan (including parking/loading/alleyway camera coverage, acceptable-ID-forms list, and quarterly panic-alarm test protocol).
4. Obtain a binding commitment letter (or binder) for the $500,000 cannabis crime/theft policy from Canopy Risk Underwriters (or a contingency carrier); bind Employer's Liability at $1M; confirm AM Best ratings and 30-day notice provisions; obtain City additional-insured endorsements.
5. Obtain a formal bonding-capacity commitment letter from Thornfield Surety Group (or an LOC commitment from Ridgeline Community Bank) dated within 60 days of submission.
6. Hold a third community-engagement session with ≥14-day newspaper and website notice; include a telephone number and "adult-use and medical" in the notice; attach publisher's affidavit and website-posting confirmations.
7. Commission a fresh (≤60-day) buffer survey using nearest-pedestrian-route measurement, property-line to property-line, identifying each sensitive use by name and address.
8. Assemble entity-formation documents (Articles, Operating Agreement, fresh Certificate of Good Standing, IRS EIN letter, ownership chart).
9. Execute City background-check authorizations and complete Live Scan fingerprinting for Okonkwo and Ramachandran; complete Form Q14A/Q14B disclosures.
10. Prepare standalone Diversity & Inclusion and Environmental Sustainability Plans; formalize the social-equity hiring pipeline (named partners, timeline, contingency).
11. Assemble the social-equity documentation packet (R3 residency proof with 24-month duration and census-tract verification; expungement court order; signed 50%-hiring and 2-year-mentorship commitments).
12. File the special-use permit application with the ZBA; record the case number in Form Q3.
13. Identify specific community-benefit beneficiary organizations.
14. Reconcile all data inconsistencies (Section 6) across documents.

**Before ~July 23, 2024:**

15. Petition IDFPR for a conditional-license extension (up to 6 months) using Form CR-EXT-01, to protect against the September 21, 2024 expiration.

**At/after City Council approval (pre-issuance, within 30 days):**

16. Pay the $15,000 annual permit fee; obtain the $50,000 bond/LOC in City Attorney-approved form (with permit-term-plus-one-year duration); submit final insurance certificates; complete build-out and obtain the Certificate of Occupancy; install and verify security systems; verify employee Agent ID Cards; execute the community benefit agreement; obtain final special-use approval.

**Ongoing (post-issuance):**

17. Implement quarterly self-audits (due 15 days after each quarter end), annual audited financials, annual workforce demographics report, 24-hour incident reporting, 5-year records retention, annual permit renewal (60–90 days before expiration), continuous insurance with 30-day change notices, annual odor-system recertification, State-license maintenance with 24-hour notice, and the 50% R3 workforce and 2-year mentorship obligations with annual re-verification.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Greenleaf Therapeutics LLC presents a substantively developed application supported by a detailed business plan, a favorable lease, a valid State conditional license, completed community-engagement sessions, and a credible social-equity narrative. However, the package is **not yet submission-ready**. The application form is unexecuted; the odor-mitigation plan lacks the mandatory P.E. certification and engineered specifications; the security plan is unfinished; the cannabis crime/theft insurance and bonding-capacity evidence are not in place; the second community-engagement session was under-noticed; the buffer survey is stale and uses the wrong measurement methodology; and the conditional-license expiration creates a real timeline risk. Standalone diversity and environmental plans, the social-equity documentation packet, entity-formation documents, and City background-check authorizations remain outstanding. Finally, multiple conflicts among the Code, the Guide, and the Form — and several internal data inconsistencies — must be reconciled before filing. A completeness audit also identified additional items not addressed in the initial package: the floor plan's omission of restrooms (§9-5-5(B)(6); §9-5-2(O)), the material-change notification duty during pendency (§9-5-5(D)), the public-hearing 500-foot owner mailing (§9-5-7(D)), the Experience & Qualifications scoring category and tiebreaker (§9-5-10(C)(7),(D)), the four-permit cap (§9-5-4(B)), the insurance-lapse response protocol (§9-5-16(E)), the prohibited-acts and inspection obligations (§§9-5-21, 9-5-23), the waiver framework (§9-5-26), and the Form Q10/Q14C prior-license disclosures. Curing the items in Sections 4 and 7 will bring the package into compliance with Article 5 and the Submission Guide.

**[CITY OF EVANSTON, ILLINOIS]{.underline}**

**[MUNICIPAL CODE]{.underline}**

**Title 9 --- Licenses, Permits, and Business Regulations**

**Article 5 --- Cannabis Business Establishments**

Adopted by the City Council of the City of Evanston, Cook County, Illinois, Ordinance No. 78-O-23, effective January 1, 2024, as amended. This Article is adopted pursuant to the authority granted to the City of Evanston under the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act, 410 ILCS 705/, and in the exercise of the City\'s home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970. Codified at Title 9, Chapter 9, Article 5 of the Evanston City Code.

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[Section 9-5-1. Title and Purpose]{.underline}**

**(A)** This Article shall be known and may be cited as the \"City of Evanston Cannabis Business Establishments Ordinance.\"

**(B)** The purpose of this Article is to establish comprehensive local regulations governing the permitting, licensing, operation, and oversight of cannabis business establishments within the corporate limits of the City of Evanston, consistent with and supplementary to the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705/) and the implementing rules and regulations adopted thereunder by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation, the Illinois Department of Agriculture, and other applicable State agencies.

**(C)** The specific goals and objectives of this Article are as follows:

> \(1\) To protect the public health, safety, and general welfare of the residents and visitors of the City of Evanston;
>
> \(2\) To prevent the sale, distribution, or dispensing of cannabis and cannabis-infused products to persons under twenty-one (21) years of age;
>
> \(3\) To promote social equity and ensure that individuals and communities disproportionately impacted by cannabis prohibition and enforcement have meaningful opportunities to participate in the legal cannabis industry within the City;
>
> \(4\) To minimize adverse impacts of cannabis business establishments on surrounding residential neighborhoods, commercial districts, schools, daycare facilities, and other sensitive land uses;
>
> \(5\) To ensure the orderly and responsible integration of cannabis business establishments into the commercial fabric of the City of Evanston, in a manner that complements existing land uses and contributes positively to the economic vitality of the community;
>
> \(6\) To establish clear and transparent standards for the application, evaluation, issuance, renewal, and revocation of local cannabis business establishment permits;
>
> \(7\) To promote environmental sustainability in the operation of cannabis business establishments; and
>
> \(8\) To generate revenue for the City of Evanston that supports community reinvestment, restorative justice programs, workforce development, and public health initiatives, particularly in communities designated as Restorative, Reinvest, and Renew (R3) zones.

**(D)** This Article shall be liberally construed to effectuate the foregoing purposes. In the event of any ambiguity, the provisions of this Article shall be interpreted in favor of protecting public health and safety.

**[Section 9-5-2. Definitions]{.underline}**

For the purposes of this Article, the following terms shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this Section. """Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

For example, a dispensary with one thousand six hundred (1,600) square feet of retail floor area shall have a maximum customer occupancy of forty (40) customers in the retail floor area at any one time. The permit holder shall implement procedures to monitor and enforce the maximum occupancy limit, including a queuing system in the vestibule or waiting area if necessary. The maximum customer occupancy shall be posted conspicuously at the entrance to the retail floor area.

**(K) Quarterly Compliance Self-Audits.** Every permit holder shall conduct a comprehensive compliance self-audit at the end of each calendar quarter, as defined in Section 9-5-2 of this Article. The self-audit shall review the permit holder\'s compliance with all provisions of this Article, all conditions of the permit, all applicable provisions of the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act and the implementing regulations, and any additional conditions imposed by the City Council. The self-audit shall include, at a minimum, a review of security system functionality, inventory accuracy, employee licensing status, insurance and bonding compliance, record-keeping practices, odor mitigation system performance, and community benefit obligations.

The results of each quarterly self-audit, including a description of all areas reviewed, all identified deficiencies, the corrective actions taken or planned to address each deficiency, and the timeline for completion of corrective actions, shall be submitted in writing to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator within fifteen (15) calendar days following the end of the applicable calendar quarter. For purposes of this subsection, the applicable calendar quarters and their corresponding submission deadlines are as follows:

> Quarter 3 (July 1 through September 30): Self-audit report due by October 15;
